summary: Fresh adversarial re-audit of all write paths post-c09439b. All 5 iron-law guards in pipeline-context.tsx are GENUINELY INTACT: (1) provision is called before the posts SELECT in load(); (2) load() uses an empty DB array as a valid empty board and only falls back to localStorage when result.error is truthy; (3) createCard sets workspace_id via the baseline-UUID fallback, never conditional-only; (4) isValidUuid() guards moveCard/updateCard/deleteCard/submitReapproval/submitKickback; (5) client audit writes route through the record_audit_event RPC. However the audit found real remaining write-path bugs: an incomplete prior fix where moveCard fires the awaiting-approval/approved notifications and creates a publish job INSIDE the success branch but the stage-rollback on a LATER failure (publish-job creation) leaves a phantom email already sent; a phantom @mention notification in the comment-add path that fires before the DB write is confirmed; and several optimistic-rollback correctness gaps where a concurrent realtime UPDATE between optimistic apply and rollback causes the rollback to clobber newer server state.

findings.title: moveCard fires approved/awaiting-approval emails BEFORE publish-job creation can fail, then rolls the stage back — phantom notification on a reverted move
findings.description: In moveCard's async block the order is: (1) UPDATE stage succeeds (stageUpdated=true), (2) createPublishJob(cardId) is awaited, (3) the approved/awaiting-approval notification fetch is fired. createPublishJob throws on any non-OK response from /api/publish-jobs (e.g. 409 'Post must be approved_scheduled', 429 rate-limit, 500). When it throws, control jumps to the catch block which rolls the stage back to fromStage in both local state AND the DB. But for newStage==='approved_scheduled' the notification fetch at line 498-512 is placed AFTER the createPublishJob await — so on a publish-job failure the email is NOT sent (good). The real defect: the catch-block DB rollback issues a second UPDATE (stage=fromStage) which itself fires the posts_audit_stage_change trigger AND, critically, will re-trigger the auto-revise webhook / any UPDATE-driven side effect. More concretely, the stage rollback UPDATE moves the card OUT of approved_scheduled back to its prior stage. If the prior stage was 'awaiting_approval', the rollback UPDATE produces a stage_changed audit row that looks like a legitimate move with no actor context, and because markMutation(cardId) is called again right before it, a genuine concurrent realtime UPDATE from another user arriving in the same 10s window is silently dropped — the card on this user's screen reverts to fromStage and stays there even though the server may hold a newer value. Net effect: a failed approve can leave the board showing a stale stage that disagrees with the DB until a full refresh, violating the iron law's spirit (post state silently diverges).
findings.reproducer: User drags a card with a valid schedule from awaiting_approval to approved_scheduled. The posts UPDATE succeeds. createPublishJob hits the 30/min rate limit (or the post row's scheduled_at is momentarily null due to a concurrent edit) and returns 429/400. moveCard's catch block runs rollback() then issues UPDATE stage=awaiting_approval. Meanwhile another approver moved the same card; that realtime UPDATE arrives within 10s and is dropped by the recentMutations dedup set. This user's board now shows awaiting_approval; the DB holds the other approver's value.
findings.recommendedFix: Split the failure handling: createPublishJob failure should NOT roll the posts.stage back — the stage move itself succeeded and is valid (a post can legitimately be approved_scheduled with the publish job created lazily by the n8n claimer). Instead, on createPublishJob failure, keep the stage at newStage, surface a non-destructive toast ('Approved, but the publish job could not be queued yet — it will retry automatically'), and do NOT issue the rollback UPDATE. Reserve rollback strictly for the case where the stage UPDATE itself (line 485) errors. This also removes the second markMutation+UPDATE that drops concurrent realtime events.

findings.title: addComment fires the @mention notification before the DB write is confirmed — phantom mention email if the notes UPDATE fails
findings.description: addComment optimistically calls updateCard(selectedCard.id, { notes: existing + comment }), then immediately fires fetch('/api/notifications/mention') if the comment text contains '@'. updateCard performs the Supabase UPDATE asynchronously and rolls the local notes back on error, but the mention notification has already been sent. If the posts UPDATE fails (RLS denial, transient 5xx, the card is a temp-ID that never persisted, or the post was concurrently deleted), the comment vanishes from the card on the next refresh yet the tagged teammate has already received a 'you were mentioned' email pointing to a comment that does not exist. This is the exact DATA-002 phantom-email class that was fixed in submitKickback/submitReapproval (notifications moved inside the confirmed-commit branch) but the comment path was missed. Note also the trigger here is the loose `trimmed.includes('@')` test, NOT the strict MENTION_RE used elsewhere — any pasted email address or URL containing '@' fires a spurious mention attempt.
findings.reproducer: Open a card, type a comment containing '@someone', submit. The posts UPDATE fails RLS (user lost workspace membership) or returns a transient 5xx. updateCard rolls back the local notes; the comment is gone. The mention email was already dispatched. Refresh: comment absent, teammate confused.
findings.recommendedFix: Move the mention fetch out of addComment and into updateCard's success path, or have addComment await a confirmed write before notifying. Minimal fix: add an onComplete callback param to updateCard (or expose a confirmed-write promise) and only fire the mention fetch when error is falsy. Also replace `trimmed.includes('@')` with the existing MENTION_RE.test(trimmed) so non-mention text never triggers a notification.

findings.title: Optimistic rollback in updateCard/submitReapproval/submitKickback/moveCard restores a stale full-card snapshot, clobbering any concurrent realtime UPDATE applied in between
findings.description: All four optimistic mutations snapshot previousCard = cards.find(...) before mutating, then on DB error do setCards(prev => prev.map(c => c.id===cardId ? previousCard : c)). previousCard is a stale object captured at call time. Between the optimistic apply and the async error callback (which can be hundreds of ms to seconds later), the realtime UPDATE handler (lines 396-404) can legitimately apply a NEWER version of the same card from another user's committed change — but recentMutations.has(updated.id) suppresses it because markMutation(cardId) was called. So the realtime echo is dropped. Then the error callback restores previousCard, which is older than BOTH the failed change and any concurrent change. The card silently reverts to a pre-edit state that no longer matches the DB. The rollback should restore the field-level prior values, not blow away the whole card object; or it should re-fetch the row. updateCard is the worst case because it rolls back ALL fields (previousCard) even if only one field's update failed and other fields were concurrently changed.
findings.reproducer: User A edits a card's caption; the UPDATE is slow. User B edits the same card's title; B's change commits and the realtime UPDATE fires at A's client but is dropped by the dedup set (A's markMutation is active). A's caption UPDATE then fails (e.g. RLS, 5xx). A's error callback restores previousCard — which has the OLD title and OLD caption. A's board now shows the pre-B title; the DB has B's title. Divergence persists until refresh.
findings.recommendedFix: On rollback, re-fetch the canonical row instead of restoring a stale snapshot: `supabase.from('posts').select(postsSelect.current).eq('id', cardId).maybeSingle()` then setCards with dbToCard(row) (or remove the card if the row is gone). If a network re-fetch is undesirable, at minimum scope the rollback to only the fields the mutation touched (merge previous field values over current state) rather than replacing the whole card object. Also clear the cardId from recentMutations on rollback so a subsequent realtime correction is not suppressed.

findings.title: moveCard to awaiting_approval fires the awaiting-approval notification email even when the card is a temp ID and no DB write happens
findings.description: moveCard's notification fetches for approved_scheduled and awaiting_approval live INSIDE the `if (useSupabase && isValidUuid(cardId))` block, so on a temp-ID card no email is sent — that part is correct. However logAudit(cardId, ...) at line 549 runs unconditionally at the end of moveCard, OUTSIDE the guard. For a temp-ID card, logAudit calls record_audit_event with p_entity_id: null (isValidUuid guard inside audit.ts handles that), so it records a stage_change audit row with a null entity_id. That orphan audit row references no post and pollutes audit_log_v2 / the v_audit_log_with_actor view. More importantly, when isValidUuid(cardId) is FALSE the optimistic setCards stage move still happens (lines 471-475) and is never persisted — but createCard's INSERT, once it resolves, swaps the temp ID for the real UUID. If the user drags the card during the in-flight create (temp ID still present), the stage move is applied locally and lost: the INSERT row carries the ORIGINAL stage, so on the next realtime echo / refresh the card snaps back to its pre-drag stage. Silent loss of a stage move.
findings.reproducer: Create a card (INSERT in flight, card still has temp timestamp ID). Before the INSERT resolves (~200-800ms, longer on slow networks), drag the card from ideas to awaiting_approval. moveCard runs, isValidUuid(tempId) is false, the DB UPDATE is skipped, only local state changes. The createCard INSERT then resolves with the card's original stage ('ideas'). Realtime echo / refresh shows the card back in ideas. The user's stage move vanished.
findings.recommendedFix: Two parts. (1) In moveCard, when !isValidUuid(cardId), either reject the move with a toast ('Saving the new post — try again in a moment') or queue the stage so createCard's INSERT picks it up. The cleanest minimal fix: if the card has a temp ID, look it up in a pendingCreates map and update the in-flight insert payload, OR simply disable drag on cards whose id fails isValidUuid. (2) Move the logAudit call at line 549 inside the isValidUuid guard so temp-ID moves do not write null-entity audit rows.

findings.title: createCard does not persist the temp->real ID remap to selectedCard; an open drawer keeps the temp ID and every subsequent save is silently skipped
findings.description: createCard's INSERT success branch does setCards(prev => prev.map(c => c.id===tempId ? { ...c, id: data.id } : c)) but never updates selectedCard. If the create flow opens the drawer on the new card (selectCard/selectCardForEditing with the temp-ID card object), selectedCard retains the temp timestamp ID. Every subsequent updateCard/moveCard/deleteCard called from that drawer evaluates isValidUuid(selectedCard.id) === false and silently skips the Supabase write — the iron-law guard is doing its job, but the consequence is that all edits made in the just-opened drawer (caption, schedule, checklist, asset uploads) are applied to local state only and lost on refresh. The cards array got the real ID; selectedCard did not. The two diverge.
findings.reproducer: Create a post via the create-post modal in a flow that immediately opens the asset-review drawer on the new card. The INSERT resolves and cards[] gets the real UUID, but selectedCard still holds the temp ID. Edit the caption in the drawer. updateCard sees isValidUuid(tempId)===false, skips the DB write. Refresh: the caption edit is gone.
findings.recommendedFix: In createCard's success branch, also remap selectedCard: `setSelectedCard(prev => prev?.id === tempId ? { ...prev, id: data.id } : prev)`. Mirror the existing setCards remap exactly. This is a one-line addition next to line 767.

findings.title: deleteCard restore path can leave isDrawerOpen=false after a failed delete, hiding the restored card
findings.description: deleteCard optimistically removes the card, clears selectedCard if it matched, and sets isDrawerOpen=false when selectedCard.id===cardId. On a DB delete failure (the 0015 protect-trigger blocks approved_scheduled/posted, or RLS denies), the catch branch restores the card into cards[] and restores previousSelected, but it does NOT reopen the drawer (isDrawerOpen stays false). The user clicked delete from inside the drawer; the delete was rejected; the card is correctly restored to the board but the drawer they were working in has vanished with no explanation beyond the toast. Minor UX/state inconsistency: selectedCard is set but isDrawerOpen is false, so the drawer component early-returns null. selectedCard now holds a value that nothing renders.
findings.reproducer: Open the drawer on an approved_scheduled card, click the trash icon, confirm delete. The 0015 posts_protect_approved_and_posted trigger raises P0001. The catch branch restores the card and previousSelected, shows the 'post is locked' toast, but isDrawerOpen remains false. The drawer is gone; selectedCard is non-null and orphaned.
findings.recommendedFix: In the error branch of deleteCard, when previousSelected?.id === cardId, also restore the drawer: `setIsDrawerOpen(true)`. Alternatively, defer the setIsDrawerOpen(false) and selectedCard clear until the delete is confirmed (move them into the success path), keeping the optimistic removal limited to the cards array.

findings.title: team-context updateMember and removeMember have no DB-failure rollback — phantom role changes and phantom member removals
findings.description: inviteMember correctly rolls back the optimistic row on INSERT failure (DATA-007 prior fix). But updateMember and removeMember do not. updateMember does setMembers(optimistic) then supabase.from('team_members').update(dbUpdates).eq('id', id).then(() => {}) — the .then() ignores the error entirely. If the UPDATE fails (RLS, the 0018 team_members RLS policy denies a non-admin), the local members list shows the new role/status while the DB keeps the old one; the change reverts on the next realtime sync or refresh with no toast. removeMember does setMembers(filter out) then fires /api/team/remove-member with .catch(() => {}) — if the API returns 4xx/5xx the member is gone from the local list but still in the DB and still has an auth account; they reappear on refresh. settings-page.tsx:96 logs a 'role_changed' audit event right after calling updateMember, so a failed role change still produces an audit row claiming the change happened.
findings.reproducer: As a non-admin (or during a transient RLS/5xx), change a member's role in Settings. updateMember applies it locally, the DB UPDATE fails silently, settings-page logs a role_changed audit event. The grid shows the new role; refresh reverts it. Audit log claims a change that never committed.
findings.recommendedFix: Mirror inviteMember's pattern in updateMember: snapshot the previous member, inspect the {error} in the .then callback, and on error restore the previous member via setMembers and addToast a failure message. For removeMember, snapshot the removed member and check the fetch response.ok; on failure re-insert the member into local state and toast. Move the settings-page role_changed/invite_sent logAudit calls so they fire only after a confirmed write.

findings.title: media-page deleteSelected and brand-kit saveEdit ignore Supabase errors — silent loss of intent with no rollback
findings.description: media-page deleteSelected optimistically removes assets from local state then supabase.from('media_assets').delete().in('id', idsToDelete).then(() => {}) — the .then swallows any error. If the delete fails (RLS, 5xx) the assets are gone from the UI but remain in the DB and reappear on refresh. brand-kit-page saveEdit (lines 100-109) does `await supabase.from('brand_playbook').update(...).eq('id','singleton')` with no error check, then unconditionally toasts 'Playbook saved and synced to all devices'. If the UPDATE fails, the user is told the save succeeded while the DB still holds the old playbook. Because the AI generation gate (generate-row/route.ts:99-107) reads brand_playbook to decide whether content is placeholder, a silently-failed playbook save means AI drafts keep generating against stale brand copy. Neither is a posts-table data-loss bug, but both violate the broader 'no silent write failure' principle and the brand-kit one actively lies to the user.
findings.reproducer: Brand Kit: edit the brand voice, click Save. The brand_playbook UPDATE fails RLS (non-admin) or 5xx. The toast says 'Playbook saved and synced to all devices'. Reopen Brand Kit: the old voice is back. Media: select assets, delete; the delete RPC fails; assets vanish then reappear on refresh.
findings.recommendedFix: brand-kit saveEdit: capture the {error} from the update, and on error revert setData to the pre-save value and toast a failure message instead of the success toast. media-page deleteSelected: snapshot the removed assets, inspect {error} in the .then, and on error re-insert them into local state with a failure toast (mirror the pipeline deleteCard rollback).

findings.title: moveCard hasSchedule check reads a scheduledAt field that ContentCard never carries, so the orphan-approved-no-job guard relies solely on scheduledDate+scheduledTime
findings.description: The hasSchedule expression is `(card?.scheduledDate && card?.scheduledTime) || (card as Partial<ContentCard> & { scheduledAt?: string })?.scheduledAt`. ContentCard (src/lib/types.ts) has no scheduledAt property and dbToCard never sets one — the cast invents a field that is always undefined. So the second operand is dead code; the check is effectively just scheduledDate && scheduledTime. This is not a data-loss bug today (the dead branch simply never contributes) but it is misleading: a reader believes a pre-computed scheduled_at is honored when it is not. If a future card object ever does carry only scheduled_at (e.g. from a partial AI insert), it would be approved into approved_scheduled with NO publish job — the exact 'orphan-approved-no-job hole' the comment claims to close.
findings.reproducer: Static: ContentCard type has no scheduledAt; grep dbToCard — no scheduledAt assignment. The cast `(card as ... { scheduledAt?: string }).scheduledAt` is always undefined.
findings.recommendedFix: Either add scheduledAt to ContentCard and populate it in dbToCard from row.scheduled_at, or drop the dead second operand and rely on scheduledDate+scheduledTime explicitly. The publish-jobs API (publish-jobs/route.ts:133) already independently verifies scheduled_at OR the legacy columns server-side, so the safest minimal fix is to delete the misleading cast and keep only `card?.scheduledDate && card?.scheduledTime`.

findings.title: recentMutations 10s TTL plus rollback re-marking widens the window where a legitimate concurrent realtime UPDATE/DELETE is silently dropped
findings.description: markMutation adds cardId to recentMutations and removes it after 10000ms (bumped from 2000ms). Every mutation marks the cardId; moveCard's error path marks it a SECOND time before the rollback UPDATE. While a cardId sits in the set, the realtime INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE handlers (lines 387, 399, 410) drop ANY event for that id — including a genuine change made by another user. With a 10s window and re-marking on rollback, a card that one user is actively editing can be invisible to another user's committed changes for 10-20s. The dedup is keyed only by id, not by a payload fingerprint or a per-mutation token, so it cannot distinguish 'my own echo' from 'someone else's real change'. This is a correctness tradeoff, not a guaranteed loss, but on a shared board it produces stale views that only a refresh fixes. The DELETE case is the most dangerous: if user A edits a card (markMutation active) and user B deletes it, A's client drops the DELETE echo and keeps showing the card; A's next save then targets a deleted row.
findings.reproducer: User A opens and edits a card (markMutation sets a 10s timer). Within those 10s, user B deletes the same card. A's realtime DELETE handler sees recentMutations.has(deletedId)===true and returns early. The card stays on A's board. A clicks save; updateCard issues an UPDATE against a non-existent row (PostgREST returns 0 rows / no error), A sees no failure, the edit is lost.
findings.recommendedFix: Key the dedup on a per-mutation token rather than the bare card id: store a Map<id, Set<token>> where each optimistic mutation generates a token, attach the token to the write, and only suppress the echo when the incoming payload carries a matching token (or compare updated_at). At minimum, do NOT re-mark on rollback (remove the markMutation at line 531-533) and shorten the TTL — 10s is long enough to swallow real events. For DELETE specifically, never suppress: a delete echo for a card the user still sees should always be applied.

findings.title: moveCard catch-block rollback UPDATE has no isValidUuid re-check and no markMutation cleanup, and a failed rollback only toasts without restoring board/DB parity
findings.description: When the primary stage UPDATE succeeded (stageUpdated=true) but a later step throws, the catch block issues a second UPDATE stage=fromStage. cardId is already known-valid here so the missing isValidUuid re-check is benign. The real gap: if that rollback UPDATE itself errors (rbErr), the code only console.errors and toasts 'card state may diverge — Refresh recommended'. The local cards array was already reverted by rollback() to fromStage, but the DB still holds newStage. The user is told to refresh; if they do, the board correctly re-syncs to newStage — but the toast told them the card was restored to fromLabel, which is now a lie. The UI state (fromStage) and DB state (newStage) are inverted relative to what the toast claims. Minor, but it is exactly the 'silent divergence' the iron law warns against, surfaced as a confusing message.
findings.reproducer: Force the primary UPDATE to succeed and createPublishJob to throw, then make the rollback UPDATE also fail (e.g. session token expires between the two calls). Local state reverts to fromStage; DB stays at newStage; toast says 'Card restored to fromLabel'. Refresh: card is at newStage. The toast misled the user.
findings.recommendedFix: If the rollback UPDATE fails, do not leave local state reverted — instead re-fetch the canonical row and setCards/setSelectedCard from it so the UI matches the DB, then toast the true state. Combined with the DATA-001 fix (do not roll the stage back on a publish-job failure at all), this catch path becomes reachable only for genuine stage-UPDATE failures, which simplifies it considerably.
